title: Getting started with RGraphSpace
text: RGraphSpace R package generates ggplot2 graphics igraph objects (Csardi Nepusz 2006), scaling nodes edges unit space. package implements new ggplot2 geometric prototypes (Wickham 2016), optimized representing large networks. enables extensive customization aesthetics visual style, including colors, shapes, line types. extended documentation use cases, see online tutorials.

title: Getting started with RGraphSpace
text: section create toy igraph object demonstrate basic RGraphSpace workflow. input data configured manually ensure users can easily view basic graph attributes. use igraph’s make_star() function create simple star-like graph V() E() functions set attributes vertices edges, respectively. RGraphSpace require vertices x, y, name attributes.
code: #--- Load required packages library("RGraphSpace") library("igraph") library("ggplot2") # Make a 'toy' igraph with 5 nodes and 4 edges; # ..either a directed or undirected graph gtoy1 <- make_star(5, mode="out")  # Check whether the graph is directed or not is_directed(gtoy1) ## [1] TRUE  # Check graph size vcount(gtoy1) ## [1] 5 ecount(gtoy1) ## [1] 4  # Assign 'x' and 'y' coordinates to each vertex; # ..this can be an arbitrary unit in (-Inf, +Inf) V(gtoy1)$x <- c(0, 2, -2, -4, -8) V(gtoy1)$y <- c(0, 0,  2, -4,  0)  # Assign a name to each vertex V(gtoy1)$name <- paste0("n", 1:5) # Plot the 'gtoy1' using standard R graphics plot(gtoy1) # Plot the 'gtoy1' using RGraphSpace plotGraphSpace(gtoy1, add.labels = TRUE)

title: Getting started with RGraphSpace
code: # Node size (numeric in [0, 100], as '%' of the plot space) V(gtoy1)$nodeSize <- c(8, 5, 5, 10, 5)  # Node shape (integer code between 0 and 25; see 'help(points)') V(gtoy1)$nodeShape <- c(21, 22, 23, 24, 25)  # Node color (Hexadecimal or color name) V(gtoy1)$nodeColor <- c("red", "#00ad39", "grey80", "lightblue", "cyan")  # Node line width (as in 'lwd' standard graphics; see 'help(gpar)') V(gtoy1)$nodeLineWidth <- 1  # Node line color (Hexadecimal or color name) V(gtoy1)$nodeLineColor <- "grey20"  # Node labels ('NA' will omit the label) V(gtoy1)$nodeLabel <- c("V1", "V2", "V3", "V4", "V5")  # Node label size (in pts) V(gtoy1)$nodeLabelSize <- 8  # Node label color (Hexadecimal or color name) V(gtoy1)$nodeLabelColor <- "black"  # Node transparency (in [0,1]) V(gtoy1)$nodeAlpha <- 1

title: Getting started with RGraphSpace
text: Given list edges, RGraphSpace represents one edge pair connected vertices. multiple edges connecting node pair, display attributes first occurrence data.
code: # Edge width (as in 'lwd' standard graphics; see 'help(gpar)') E(gtoy1)$edgeLineWidth <- 0.8  # Edge color (Hexadecimal or color name) E(gtoy1)$edgeLineColor <- c("red","green","blue","black")  # Edge type (as in 'lty' standard graphics; see 'help(gpar)') E(gtoy1)$edgeLineType <- c("solid", "11", "dashed", "2124")  # Edge transparency (in [0,1]) E(gtoy1)$edgeAlpha <- 1

title: Getting started with RGraphSpace
text: Arrowhead directed graphs: default, arrow drawn edge according left--right orientation edge list (e.g. -> B). mutual connections, package recode mutual edges represent bidirectional flow. Arrowhead undirected graphs: default, arrow drawn undirected graphs. However, arrowheads may assigned according coding . … plot updated igraph object. straightforward call simply passes igraph directly wrapper plotGraphSpace() function.
code: # Arrowhead types in directed graphs ## Integer or character code: ## 0 = "---", 1 = "-->", -1 = "--|" E(gtoy1)$arrowType <- 1 # Arrowhead types in undirected graphs ## Integer or character code: ##  0 = "---" ##  1 = "-->",  2 = "<--",  3 = "<->",  4 = "|->", ## -1 = "--|", -2 = "|--", -3 = "|-|", -4 = "<-|",  E(gtoy1)$arrowType <- 1 # Note: in undirected graphs, this attribute overrides  # the edge's orientation in the edge list # Plot the updated 'gtoy1' using RGraphSpace plotGraphSpace(gtoy1, add.labels = TRUE)

title: Getting started with RGraphSpace
text: Alternatively, igraph can converted GraphSpace object passed directly ggplot2 geoms. resulting GraphSpace integrates ggplot2, allowing graph-specific geoms combined standard ggplot2 layers.
code: # Load the toy graph used in the previous example data("gtoy1", package = 'RGraphSpace')  # Create a GraphSpace object gs <- GraphSpace(gtoy1) #> Validating the 'igraph' object... #> Creating a 'GraphSpace' object...  # Normalize the coordinates gs <- normalizeGraphSpace(gs) #> Normalizing node coordinates to graph space... ggplot(gs) +    geom_edgespace() +    geom_nodespace() +    geom_text(aes(x = x, y = y, label = nodeLabel), size = 2) +    theme_gspace_coords(is_norm = TRUE)

title: Customizing Aesthetics
text: section illustrates RGraphSpace integrates ggplot2 using geoms building blocks (Wickham 2016). Graph attributes stored within GraphSpace object can handled two ways: Identity mapping: Graph attributes interpreted “identity values” (nodeColor, nodeSize, nodeShape) displayed exactly , without scaling mapping. Dynamic aesthetic mapping: Graph attributes mapped aesthetics (colour, size, shape) rendered standard ggplot2 scales, automatically generate synchronized legends. facilitate integration, RGraphSpace implements three specialized geoms designed handle graph data types within ggplot2 workflow. geoms use dual-anchor normalization approach align layers, critical analysis network elements must stay accurately referenced spatial map image. geom_graphspace(): high-level convenience layer processes nodes edges single call. geom_nodespace(): Dedicated rendering nodes. Inherits GeomPoint aesthetic mappings, modified inform edge layer node states. geom_edgespace(): Handles relational data nodes. Inherits GeomSegment aesthetic mappings; unlike standard segments, “node-aware” dynamically calibrates start end points.

title: Customizing Aesthetics
text: following example, create small modular graph containing variables different types demonstrate RGraphSpace geoms handle different mapping requirements.
code: library("RGraphSpace") library("igraph") library("ggplot2")  # Make a toy modular graph gtoy2 <- sample_islands(   islands.n = 3,       # number of modules   islands.size = 30,   # nodes per module   islands.pin = 0.25,  # probability of edges within modules   n.inter = 2)         # edges between modules  # Assign module membership to nodes V(gtoy2)$module <- rep(1:3, each = 30)  # Assign colors to nodes V(gtoy2)$nodeColor <- rainbow(3)[V(gtoy2)$module]  # Assign a categorical variable to nodes V(gtoy2)$node_group <- c("A", "B", "C")[V(gtoy2)$module]  # Assign numeric variables to nodes and edges V(gtoy2)$node_var <- runif(vcount(gtoy2)) E(gtoy2)$edge_var <- runif(ecount(gtoy2))  # Create a GraphSpace from the toy igraph gs <- GraphSpace(gtoy2)

title: Customizing Aesthetics
text: example, nodeColor already contains final colour values stored GraphSpace object. colours displayed -geom_graphspace() function. approach useful nodes pre-processed specific color schemes want visual output without mapping.  trade-approach , one hand, attributes reflect original data directly, legend accessible. identity scales bypass legend-building process ggplot2. legend required explain meaning colors, attribute mapped via aesthetics (e.g., aes(fill = attribute)) modified scale_*() function.
code: ggplot() +    geom_graphspace(colour = "grey", data = gs) +   theme(aspect.ratio = 1)

title: Customizing Aesthetics
text: example, node categorical variable node_group mapped fill aesthetic. call geom_edgespace() geom_nodespace() independent layers provide maximum flexibility mapping aesthetics legends.
code: ggplot(gs) +    geom_edgespace() +   geom_nodespace(aes(fill = node_group), colour = "grey") +   scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "viridis") +   theme_gspace_coords()

title: Customizing Aesthetics
text: example, node edge numeric variables mapped fill colour aesthetics, respectively.
code: # Map aesthetics to numeric variables ggplot(gs) +    geom_edgespace(aes(colour = edge_var)) +   geom_nodespace(aes(fill = node_var), colour = "grey") +   scale_colour_continuous(palette = c("cyan","blue")) +   scale_fill_continuous(palette = c("white","purple")) +   theme_gspace_coords()

title: Customizing Aesthetics
text: need map different variables aesthetic (colour) independent scales, ggnewscale package offers elegant solution introduce additional scales within plot (Campitelli 2025); example:
code: if (!require("ggnewscale", quietly = TRUE)) {   install.packages("ggnewscale") } library("ggnewscale") ggplot(data = gs) +    geom_edgespace(aes(colour = edge_var)) +   scale_colour_continuous(palette = c("cyan","blue")) +   ggnewscale::new_scale_colour() +   geom_nodespace(aes(colour = node_var),      stroke = 2, fill = NA) +   scale_colour_continuous(palette = c("white","purple")) +   theme_gspace_coords()

title: Extending ggplot2 Grammar to High-Dimensional Data
text: use pbmc3k dataset SeuratData package, consisting single-cell transcriptomics data peripheral blood mononuclear cells. dataset commonly used showcase Seurat workflows (Hao et al. 2024).
code: # Install a Seurat dataset (required only once) SeuratData::InstallData("pbmc3k") # Check manifest of installed datasets # SeuratData::InstalledData()  # Load the 'pbmc3k' dataset # Note: LoadData() may print conversion warnings when loading pbmc3k. # These are expected and come from SeuratData's internal v4-to-v5 # object migration — they can be safely ignored. seurat_obj <- LoadData("pbmc3k", type = "pbmc3k.final") ## Common Seurat preprocessing workflow. ## Shown for reference only, as the 'pbmc3k'  ## dataset has already been preprocessed. # seurat_obj <- NormalizeData(seurat_obj) # seurat_obj <- ScaleData(seurat_obj) # seurat_obj <- FindVariableFeatures(seurat_obj) # seurat_obj <- RunPCA(seurat_obj) # seurat_obj <- RunUMAP(seurat_obj, dims = 1:30)

title: Extending ggplot2 Grammar to High-Dimensional Data
text: introducing ggplot-GraphSpace interface, first reproduce two typical Seurat visualizations: cluster-level embedding (DimPlot) feature expression map (FeaturePlot). examples provide useful baseline corresponding visualizations constructed later ggplot-GraphSpace.  Note DimPlot FeaturePlot high-level functions. Internally, Seurat extracts relevant data generates ggplot objects. convenient, underlying data structures directly exposed user, making advanced use ggplot2 grammar difficult. example, defining custom aesthetic mappings interoperating visualization workflows require extra data extraction steps.
code: cpal <- DiscretePalette(nlevels(seurat_obj$seurat_annotations),    palette = "polychrome")  # Left: cluster-level embedding p1 <- DimPlot(seurat_obj, pt.size = 1, cols = cpal) +    theme_minimal() + theme(aspect.ratio = 1) p1  # Right: feature expression map p2 <- FeaturePlot(seurat_obj, features = "LYZ",    cols = c("lightgrey", "blue"), pt.size = 1) +    theme_minimal() + theme(aspect.ratio = 1) p2  # Plot side-by-side # p1 + p2

text: now apply .GraphSpace() expose Seurat object’s underlying high-dimensional data directly ggplot2 (additional details, see coercing high-dimensional data section). GraphSpace object ready, reproduce plots using ggplot2 geoms aesthetic mappings.  resulting plots essentially , users now full flexibility interact directly ggplot2 rich ecosystem extensions companion packages.
code: # Create a GraphSpace from 'seurat_obj' gs <- as.GraphSpace(seurat_obj, space = "embedding", reduction = "umap")  # Seurat object converted to GraphSpace: # ℹ space=embedding, layer=default, features=13714, samples=2638, reduction="umap" # Node spatial boundaries: # ℹ x: [-9, 13] (cols) # ℹ y: [-9, 14] (rows) gs # A GraphSpace-class object for: # IGRAPH 1393201 UN-- 2638 0 --  # + attr: x (v/n), y (v/n), name (v/c), nodeLabel (v/c), nodeSize (v/n), orig.ident (v/x), # | nCount_RNA (v/n), nFeature_RNA (v/n), seurat_annotations (v/x), percent.mt (v/n), # | RNA_snn_res.0.5 (v/x), seurat_clusters (v/x), arrowType (e/n) # + features: 13714 (AL627309.1, AP006222.2, RP11-206L10.2, RP11-206L10.9, ...) cpal <- DiscretePalette(nlevels(gs$seurat_annotations),    palette = "polychrome")  # Left: cluster-level embedding p3 <- ggplot(gs) +    geom_nodespace(mapping = aes(fill = seurat_annotations),     size = 1.5, color = "grey90", stroke = 0.3) +   scale_fill_manual(values = cpal) +   labs(x = "UMAP_1", y = "UMAP_2") +   theme_gspace_legend(discrete_fill = TRUE) +   theme_minimal() + theme(aspect.ratio = 1) p3  # Right: feature expression map p4 <- ggplot(gs) +    geom_nodespace(mapping = aes(fill = LYZ),      size = 1.5, color = "lightgrey", stroke = 0.3) +   scale_fill_continuous(palette = c("lightgrey", "blue")) +   labs(x = "UMAP_1", y = "UMAP_2")  +   theme_minimal() + theme(aspect.ratio = 1) p4  # Plot side-by-side # p3 + p4

title: Extending ggplot2 Grammar to High-Dimensional Data
text: .GraphSpace() function provides convenient way coerce high-dimensional data GraphSpace object. However, coercion method can anticipate every possible data structure. , show access relevant components Seurat object use construct GraphSpace manually. another coercion example, see spatial data tutorial.
code: # Extract UMAP embeddings as node coordinates coords <- Embeddings(seurat_obj, reduction = "umap") coords <- coords[, seq_len(2)] |> as.data.frame() colnames(coords) <- c("x", "y")  # Extract cell metadata metadata <- seurat_obj[[]]  # Merge coordinates and metadata using common cell identifiers ids <- intersect(rownames(coords), rownames(metadata)) coords <- cbind(coords[ids, ], metadata[ids, ])  # Construct a GraphSpace object # Metadata become node attributes gs <- GraphSpace(coords)  # Add high-dimensional feature data # Stored separately for lazy aesthetic mapping gs_fdata(gs) <- SeuratObject::LayerData(seurat_obj, layer = "data")  # Optional: normalize node coordinates gs <- normalizeGraphSpace(gs, mar = 0.01)

title: Interactive visualization
text: jumping interactive interface, can use standard RGraphSpace call verify graph’s structure current layout. provides baseline comparison start modifying network dynamically.  main advantage interoperability comes “round-trip” workflow. can send graph space RedeR, use force-directed algorithms manually refine layout, pull updated geometry back R analysis plotting ggplot2. following steps demonstrate launch, manipulation, retrieval process:
code: library("RGraphSpace") library("RedeR") library("igraph") data(gtoy1, package = "RGraphSpace") plotGraphSpace(gtoy1, add.labels = TRUE) # Launch the RedeR application startRedeR() resetRedeR()  # Send 'gtoy1' to the RedeR interface addGraphToRedeR(gtoy1, unit="npc") relaxRedeR()  # Fetch 'gtoy1' with a fresh layout gtoy1_2 <- getGraphFromRedeR(unit="npc")  # Check the round trip... plotGraphSpace(gtoy1_2, add.labels = TRUE)  ## Note that for the round trip, shapes and line types are ## partially compatible between ggplot2 and RedeR.  # ...alternatively, just update the graph layout gtoy1_2 <- updateLayoutFromRedeR(g=gtoy1)  # ...check the updated layout plotGraphSpace(gtoy1_2, add.labels = TRUE)

code: # Make a large modular graph nmod = 20 size = 50 gtoy2 <- sample_islands(   islands.n = nmod,     # number of modules   islands.size = size,  # nodes per module   islands.pin = 0.25,   # edges within modules (prob)   n.inter = 5)          # edges between modules  # Assign module membership V(gtoy2)$module <- rep(seq_len(nmod), each = size)  # Assign colors and node size V(gtoy2)$nodeColor <- rainbow(nmod)[V(gtoy2)$module] V(gtoy2)$nodeSize <- 2    # Initialize a 'GraphSpace', using 'layout_with_kk' to  # generate initial global coordinates gs_gtoy2 <- GraphSpace(gtoy2, layout = layout_with_kk(gtoy2))  plotGraphSpace(gs_gtoy2)

code: # Launch the RedeR application startRedeR() resetRedeR()  # Send 'gtoy2' to the RedeR interface addGraphToRedeR(gs_graph(gs_gtoy2), unit="npc")  #--- Fine-tune the force-directed layout: # p1: edge target length (default = 100) # p2: edge stiffness (default = 100) # p5: node movement limit (default = 100) relaxRedeR(p1 = 10, p2 = 50, p5 = 1)  # Update the graph layout gtoy2_2 <- updateLayoutFromRedeR(g = gs_graph(gs_gtoy2))  # ...check the updated layout plotGraphSpace(gtoy2_2, add.labels = FALSE)

title: Interoperability with 'ggraph' and 'sf'
text: RGraphSpace designed seamless extension existing network analysis workflows, replacement. Whether using igraph heavy-duty computations tidygraph tidy data manipulation, RGraphSpace geoms automatically recognize objects fly. main motivation behind RGraphSpace address challenge scaling network elements without disrupting alignment image features. practical examples, see mapping graphs images; see also PathwaySpace tutorials use-case scenarios involving reference image backgrounds. use RGraphSpace ggraph? ggraph wonderful framework relational data, precise edge-node alignment requires additional handling node sizes vary dynamically. limitation arises fundamental trade-ggplot2: scaling point size aesthetic tied fixed physical legend representation, causing node dimensions depend device scaling rather normalized coordinate space. applications issue, becomes critical graphs must spatially aligned reference images. RGraphSpace addresses specialized geoms automatically compensate alignment shifts introduced node scaling. trade-higher level automation user fewer customization options compared ggraph approach. exactly using RGraphSpace alongside ggraph makes sense: provides precise spatial alignment graph elements reference backgrounds preserving interoperability extensive layout styling flexibility ggraph grammar.

title: Interoperability with 'ggraph' and 'sf'
text: following example demonstrates interoperability RGraphSpace ggraph using igraph tidygraph objects, managing spatial data sf, standard infrastructure spatial data analysis R (Pebesma Bivand 2023). Integrating network structures spatial data often creates headache mismatched coordinate systems scales, makes example particularly interesting showcase packages handle complexity. Next, build spatial network cities; RGraphSpace geoms plugged ggraph sf workflows.
code: # Load a map and transform projection map_sf <- st_as_sf(map("world", regions = "Brazil", fill = TRUE))  # Filter major cities by regional capitals data(world.cities, package = "maps") r_capitals <- c(   "Aracaju", "Belem", "Belo Horizonte", "Boa Vista", "Brasilia",    "Campo Grande", "Cuiaba", "Curitiba", "Florianopolis", "Fortaleza",    "Goiania", "Joao Pessoa", "Macapa", "Maceio", "Manaus", "Natal",    "Palmas", "Porto Alegre", "Porto Velho", "Recife", "Rio Branco",    "Rio de Janeiro", "Salvador", "Sao Luis", "Sao Paulo", "Teresina",    "Vitoria" ) cities <- subset(world.cities, country.etc == "Brazil" &      name %in% r_capitals & pop > 1200000)  # Create Delaunay triangulation edges # Note: the edges hold no particular meaning beyond # demonstrating integration between coordinate systems tri <- delaunayn(cities[,c("lat","long")]) edges <- unique(rbind(tri[,c(1,2)], tri[,c(2,3)], tri[,c(1,3)] ))  # Build an 'igraph' using city coordinates igraph_cities <- igraph::graph_from_edgelist(edges, directed = FALSE) igraph::V(igraph_cities)$x <- cities$long igraph::V(igraph_cities)$y <- cities$lat igraph::V(igraph_cities)$Cities <- cities$name igraph::V(igraph_cities)$`Population (M)` <- cities$pop/1000000 igraph::E(igraph_cities)$arrowType <- 3

title: Interoperability with 'ggraph' and 'sf'
text: following options produce visual output, demonstrating packages integrate different types input data. Although four approaches produce visualization, Option 1 provides automatic node-edge synchronization. GraphSpace object supplied directly ggplot(), clipping metadata propagated automatically node edge layers. workflows, inject_nodespace() must called explicitly synchronize clipping offsets.
code: # Option 1: Passing a 'GraphSpace' object directly to ggplot() gs <- GraphSpace(igraph_cities) ggplot(gs) +   geom_sf(data = map_sf, fill = "grey95", color = "grey60") +   geom_edgespace(color = "grey40", arrow_size = 0.5) +   geom_nodespace(aes(fill = Cities, size = `Population (M)`)) +   scale_size(range = c(3, 9)) +   theme_gray() +   theme_gspace_legend(discrete_fill = TRUE)  # Option 2: Passing an 'igraph' object to RGraphSpace geoms ggplot() +   geom_sf(data = map_sf, fill = "grey95", color = "grey60") +   geom_edgespace(color = "grey40", arrow_size = 0.5, data = igraph_cities) +   geom_nodespace(aes(fill = Cities, size = `Population (M)`), data = igraph_cities) +   scale_size(range = c(3, 9)) +   inject_nodespace() +    theme_gray() +   theme_gspace_legend(discrete_fill = TRUE)  # Option 3: Passing a 'tbl_graph' object to RGraphSpace geoms gr <- as_tbl_graph(igraph_cities) ggplot() +   geom_sf(data = map_sf, fill = "grey95", color = "grey60") +   geom_edgespace(color = "grey40", arrow_size = 0.5, data = gr) +   geom_nodespace(aes(fill = Cities, size = `Population (M)`), data = gr) +   scale_size(range = c(3, 9)) +   inject_nodespace() +    theme_gray() +   theme_gspace_legend(discrete_fill = TRUE)  # Option 4: Integrating RGraphSpace geoms into a ggraph workflow gr <- as_tbl_graph(igraph_cities) ggraph(graph = gr, x= gr$x, y = gr$y) +   geom_sf(data = map_sf, fill = "grey95", color = "grey60") +   geom_edgespace(color = "grey40", arrow_size = 0.5) +   geom_nodespace(aes(fill = Cities, size = `Population (M)`)) +   scale_size(range = c(3, 9)) +   inject_nodespace() +   theme_gray() +   theme_gspace_legend(discrete_fill = TRUE)

title: Mapping Graphs to Images
text: example, next extract features volcano matrix map graph nodes. graph visualized image matrix.
code: library("RGraphSpace") library("igraph") library("ggplot2")  # Extract pixel coordinates for a specific intensity quantile. coords <- which(volcano == quantile(volcano, 0.85), arr.ind = TRUE)  # Mark target pixels with '0'; it will appear as black in the background.  # This creates a visual anchor to verify the alignment precision. volcano2 <- volcano volcano2[coords] <- 0  # Create an igraph object from the pixel coordinates;  # note that at this stage, 'y' represents matrix row indices. gtoy3 <- igraph::make_empty_graph(n = nrow(coords)) igraph::V(gtoy3)$y <- coords[,1] igraph::V(gtoy3)$x <- coords[,2]  # Highlight the bottom-row vertex (max 'y' index) to demonstrate alignment;  # since matrix indexing is top-down, this accounts for the default flip  # between matrix and plot coordinate systems. igraph::V(gtoy3)$nodeColor <- NA bottom_row <- which.max(igraph::V(gtoy3)$y) igraph::V(gtoy3)$nodeColor[bottom_row] <- adjustcolor("red", 0.4)

title: Fine-Tuning Scales and Offsets
text: , construct star-like network varying node sizes show geometries stay synchronized across wide range values.
code: library("RGraphSpace") library("igraph") library("ggplot2")  # Make a toy graph gtoy_star <- make_star(20, mode="out")  # Add a numeric variable V(gtoy_star)$num_var <- seq_len(vcount(gtoy_star)) / 2  # Set the 'nodeSize' attribute V(gtoy_star)$nodeSize <- seq_len(vcount(gtoy_star)) * 2  # Set node and edge colors V(gtoy_star)$nodeColor <- adjustcolor("blue", 0.1) E(gtoy_star)$edgeLineColor <- "darkred"  # Assign random arrow types, either '-->' or '--|' E(gtoy_star)$arrowType <- sample(c(1, -1), ecount(gtoy_star), replace = T)  # Make a 'GraphSpace' gs_star <- GraphSpace(gtoy_star, layout = layout_as_star(gtoy_star))

what: The problem: static vs. dynamic sizes
title: Fine-Tuning Scales and Offsets
text: first example, GraphSpace object provides graph attributes. Using predefined node sizes allows consistent arrow offsets, network elements scaled npc (Normalized Parent Coordinates) units. matter plotting area resized, nodes, edges, arrows remain proportional viewport. behavior especially useful overlaying networks top reference images (photomicrography medical scans), nodes must stay locked specific pixel positions regardless output resolution.  map node size variable (like num_var), ggplot2 rescales values target range (e.g., c(2, 40)). provides advantages ggplot2 ecosystem, flexible graphical scaling coordinated legends. , however, subtle trade-keep mind. node size mapped via aes(), ggplot2 treats fixed physical dimension (usually mm) maintain consistency legends. means node size stay locked legends longer scale proportionally plotting area resized. example , geom_graphspace() handles bulk edge adjustment, arrow_offset parameter providing additional manual fine-tuning.
code: ggplot() +    geom_graphspace(data = gs_star) +    theme_gspace_coords() ggplot() +    geom_graphspace(mapping = aes(size = num_var),     data = gs_star,  arrow_offset = 0.03) +    scale_size(range = c(2, 40)) +    theme_gspace_coords() +    theme(legend.position = "none")

title: Fine-Tuning Scales and Offsets
text: geom_graphspace() convenient quick, coordinated plots, limitation: mapping argument reserved node aesthetics. overcome limitation, can call geom_edgespace() geom_nodespace() independent layers. allows total flexibility mapping different variables edges nodes. However, layers now independent, longer “talk” default. example, node sizes modified scale transformation, edge layer direct way determine resulting node boundaries needed clipping calculations. address , RGraphSpace performs post-processing synchronization step plot construction, intercepting calculated sizes node layer “injecting” corresponding clipping information edge layer.  provide final note customization, scaling trade-offs apply size passed node aesthetic mapping. Otherwise, RGraphSpace defaults using npc units network elements.
code: # We shuffle 'num_var' to demonstrate that edges  # still find their specific boundaries set.seed(234) gs_star$num_var2 <- sample(gs_star$num_var)  # Execute independent node and edge layers ggplot(data = gs_star) +    geom_edgespace(arrow_offset = 0.03) +    geom_nodespace(mapping = aes(size = num_var2 )) +    scale_size(range = c(2, 40)) +   theme_gspace_coords() +   theme(legend.position = "none")

code: # Install a Seurat dataset (required only once) SeuratData::InstallData("stxBrain") # Check manifest of installed datasets # SeuratData::InstalledData()  # Load the 'stxBrain' dataset # Note: LoadData() may print conversion warnings when loading pbmc3k. # These are expected and come from SeuratData's internal v4-to-v5 # object migration — they can be safely ignored. seurat_obj <- LoadData("stxBrain", type = "anterior1")

title: Extending ggplot2 Grammar to Spatial Transcriptomics Data
text: stxBrain dataset normalized suggested Seurat’s spatial_vignette, either using SCTransform() NormalizeData() functions.
code: # NOTE: Seurat recommends using SCTransform() for processing this  # spatial dataset, which may require more computation time. Here, # we use log-normalization for demonstration purposes. seurat_obj <- NormalizeData(seurat_obj)

title: Extending ggplot2 Grammar to Spatial Transcriptomics Data
text: Next, create GraphSpace Seurat object; .GraphSpace() converts Seurat object GraphSpace, exposing spatial coordinates feature data ggplot2 grammar. attach tissue image normalize node coordinates image space.
code: # Create a GraphSpace from 'seurat_obj' gs <- as.GraphSpace(seurat_obj, space = "spatial", scale = "lowres")  # Seurat object converted to GraphSpace: # ℹ space=spatial, layer=default, features=31053, samples=2696, scale="lowres" # Node spatial boundaries: # ℹ x: [76, 493] (cols) # ℹ y: [138, 541] (rows) # If available, add tissue image  gs_image(gs) <- SeuratObject::GetImage(seurat_obj, mode = "raster")  # Image spatial boundaries: # ℹ x: [1, 600] (cols) # ℹ y: [1, 599] (rows) # Normalize node coordinates to the image space gs <- normalizeGraphSpace(gs) # Normalizing node coordinates to image space... # Flipping y-coordinates...  gs # A GraphSpace-class object for: # IGRAPH c4b5fdf UN-- 2696 0 --  # + attr: x (v/n), y (v/n), name (v/c), nodeLabel (v/c), nodeSize (v/n), cell (v/c), # | orig.ident (v/x), nCount_Spatial (v/n), nFeature_Spatial (v/n), slice (v/n), region # | (v/c), arrowType (e/n) # + features: 31053 (Xkr4, Gm1992, Gm37381, Rp1, ...)

Note image alignment: Proper spatial alignment nodes background image requires consistent coordinate conventions. Spatial misalignment may occur input image node coordinates differ axis orientation (e.g., top-left versus bottom-left origins). accommodate differences, normalizeGraphSpace() provides orientation controls rotate.xy, flip.x, flip.y arguments. nodes appear misaligned input image, try combinations parameters correct alignment. Alternatively, try flip.v flip.h arguments apply flipping directly background image.
code: cpal <- hcl.colors(100, palette = "Spectral", rev = TRUE)  # Reproduce a typical Seurat's spatial feature visualization ggplot(gs) +    annotation_gspace_image(gs) +   geom_nodespace(mapping = aes(colour = Ttr), size = 1, pch = 19) +   scale_colour_continuous(palette = cpal) +   theme_gspace_coords(theme = "th3", is_norm = TRUE,      xlab = "Tissue coordinates 1", ylab = "Tissue coordinates 2")

title: Extending ggplot2 Grammar to Spatial Transcriptomics Data
text: section requires additional preprocessing stxBrain dataset, including normalization SCTransform() Seurat’s clustering workflow. recommend installing glmGamPoi package beforehand, substantially speeds SCTransform() estimation step.

title: Extending ggplot2 Grammar to Spatial Transcriptomics Data
code: if (!require("glmGamPoi", quietly = TRUE)){   BiocManager::install("glmGamPoi") } # Run vst normalization on counts seurat_obj <- SCTransform(seurat_obj, assay = "Spatial", verbose = FALSE) seurat_obj <- RunPCA(seurat_obj, assay = "SCT", verbose = FALSE) seurat_obj <- FindNeighbors(seurat_obj, reduction = "pca", dims = 1:30) seurat_obj <- FindClusters(seurat_obj, verbose = FALSE)

title: Extending ggplot2 Grammar to Spatial Transcriptomics Data
text: clusters assigned, rebuild GraphSpace object updated seurat_obj reproduce typical Seurat spatial cluster plot, mapping cluster identity fill aesthetic overlaying tissue image dimmed background.
code: # Re-create a GraphSpace from the updated 'seurat_obj' gs <- as.GraphSpace(seurat_obj, space = "spatial", scale = "lowres") gs_image(gs) <- SeuratObject::GetImage(seurat_obj, mode = "raster") gs <- normalizeGraphSpace(gs) # Reproduce a typical Seurat cluster visualization cpal <- DiscretePalette(nlevels(gs$seurat_clusters), palette = "polychrome") ggplot(gs) +    annotation_gspace_image(gs, opacity = 0.5) +   geom_nodespace(mapping = aes(fill = seurat_clusters),     size = 1.3, color = "grey90", stroke = 0.3) +   scale_fill_manual(values = cpal) +   theme_gspace_coords(theme = "th2", is_norm = TRUE,      xlab = "Tissue coordinates 1", ylab = "Tissue coordinates 2") +   theme_gspace_legend(discrete_fill = TRUE)

title: Extending ggplot2 Grammar to Spatial Transcriptomics Data
text: , show access relevant components Seurat object use construct GraphSpace manually, without relying .GraphSpace(). another coercion example, see high-dimensional data tutorial.
code: # Extract tissue coordinates coords <- SeuratObject::GetTissueCoordinates(object = seurat_obj, scale = "lowres") coords <- as.data.frame(coords) all(c("x", "y") %in% colnames(coords)) # [1] TRUE  # Extract cell metadata metadata <- seurat_obj[[]]  # Merge coordinates and metadata using common cell identifiers ids <- intersect(rownames(coords), rownames(metadata)) coords <- cbind(coords[ids, ], metadata[ids, ])  # Construct a GraphSpace object # Metadata become node attributes gs <- GraphSpace(coords)  # Add high-dimensional feature data # Stored separately for lazy aesthetic mapping gs_fdata(gs) <- SeuratObject::LayerData(seurat_obj, layer = "data")  # If available, add tissue image  gs_image(gs) <- SeuratObject::GetImage(seurat_obj, mode = "raster")  # Normalize node coordinates to the image space gs <- normalizeGraphSpace(gs)

title: A Lightweight Interface Between igraph and ggplot2 Graphics
text: RGraphSpace R package generates ggplot2 graphics igraph objects (Csardi Nepusz 2006), scaling nodes edges unit space. package implements new ggplot2 prototypes (Wickham 2016), optimized representing large networks. enables extensive customization aesthetics visual style, including interoperability ggraph tidygraph workflows (Pedersen 2024). Three specialized geoms translate graph data geometric layers. geom_graphspace(): high-level convenience layer processes nodes edges single call. geom_nodespace(): Dedicated rendering nodes. Inherits GeomPoint aesthetic mappings, modified inform edge layer node states. geom_edgespace(): Handles relational data nodes. Inherits GeomSegment aesthetic mappings; unlike standard segments, “node-aware” dynamically calibrates start end points connected nodes.

Get a data frame with edges gs_edges(gs) #>   x y xend yend offset_start offset_end vertex1 vertex2 name1 name2 #> 1 0 0    2    0            8          5       1       2    n1    n2 #> 2 0 0   -2    2            8          5       1       3    n1    n3 #> 3 0 0   -4   -4            8         10       1       4    n1    n4 #> 4 0 0   -8    0            8          5       1       5    n1    n5 #>   edgeLineType edgeLineColor edgeLineWidth arrowType edgeAlpha #> 1        solid           red           0.8         1         1 #> 2        solid         green           0.8         1         1 #> 3       dashed          blue           0.8         1         1 #> 4        solid         black           0.8         1         1  # Get vertex count gs_vcount(gs) #> [1] 5  # Get edge count gs_ecount(gs) #> [1] 4  # Access all vertex attributes gs_vertex_attr(gs) #> $x #> [1]  0  2 -2 -4 -8 #>  #> $y #> [1]  0  0  2 -4  0 #>  #> $name #> [1] "n1" "n2" "n3" "n4" "n5" #>  #> $nodeLabel #> [1] "V1" "V2" "V3" "V4" "V5" #>  #> $nodeLabelSize #> [1] 8 8 8 8 8 #>  #> $nodeLabelColor #> [1] "black" "black" "black" "black" "black" #>  #> $nodeShape #> [1] 21 22 23 24 25 #>  #> $nodeSize #> [1]  8  5  5 10  5 #>  #> $nodeColor #> [1] "red"       "#00ad39"   "grey80"    "lightblue" "cyan"      #>  #> $nodeLineWidth #> [1] 1 1 1 1 1 #>  #> $nodeLineColor #> [1] "grey20" "grey20" "grey20" "grey20" "grey20" #>  #> $nodeAlpha #> [1] 1 1 1 1 1 #>   # title: Create a GraphSpace object — GraphSpace,ANY-method
text: GraphSpace objects designed bridge gap network analysis (via igraph) high-quality visualization (via ggplot2). constructor ensures necessary aesthetics geom_graphspace pre-processed validated. Coordinate System Normalization: default, constructor expects coordinates x y vertex attributes, along unique IDs name vertex attribute. provided, constructor generate sequential IDs assign layout using layout_nicely function. coordinates define relative positioning nodes. optimal rendering, recommended pass object normalizeGraphSpace construction. converts vertex positions Normalized Parent Coordinates (NPC), ensuring graph remains centered scaled relative plotting area. Data Structure: resulting object stores nodes edges separate internal slots, preserving metadata nodeSize edgeLineColor. igraph object provided without specific styling attributes, GraphSpace assign default values defined geom_graphspace aesthetics. Users can also specify custom variables input graph used aesthetics within ggplot2 grammar. Arrowhead Mapping: arrowType attribute (see Arrowhead types section) allows mapping symbolic aliases ("-->") internal integer codes. useful assigning interaction types directed undirected graphs (e.g., activation vs. inhibition).

code: library(RGraphSpace) library(igraph) library(ggplot2)  # Make a demo igraph gtoy1 <- make_star(15, mode="out")  # Set some node attributes V(gtoy1)$nodeSize <- runif(vcount(gtoy1), 1, 20) V(gtoy1)$nodeColor <- rainbow(vcount(gtoy1))  # Set some variables V(gtoy1)$user_var1 <- runif(vcount(gtoy1), 1, 3)^3 V(gtoy1)$user_var2 <-  rep(c(1, 2, 3), each = 5)  # Create a GraphSpace object gs <- GraphSpace(gtoy1, layout = layout_in_circle(gtoy1)) #> Validating the 'igraph' object... #> Vertex attribute 'name' missing; assigning names...  #> Creating a 'GraphSpace' object...  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{  # Example 1: Nodes scaling with the legend # When 'size' is mapped inside aes(), it follows # ggplot2 default behavior: size is translated  # to absolute units (mm) via 'scale_size()'.  ggplot() +    geom_graphspace(   mapping = aes(size = nodeSize, fill = user_var2),    data = gs, arrow_offset = 0.01) +    scale_size(range = c(1, 12)) +    theme(aspect.ratio = 1)    # Example 2: Nodes scaling with the viewport # When 'size' is passed as a node attribute,  # inherited from the igraph object, it is # interpreted as a percentage of the plotting  # area and translated to NPC units.  ggplot() +   geom_graphspace(mapping = aes(fill = user_var2),    data = gs, arrow_offset = 0.01) +    theme(aspect.ratio = 1)    } # }

code: library(RGraphSpace) library(igraph) library(ggplot2)  # Generate a toy star graph gtoy1 <- make_star(15, mode = "out") V(gtoy1)$my_node_var <- runif(vcount(gtoy1), 1, 20)  # Create a GraphSpace object gs <- GraphSpace(gtoy1, layout = layout_in_circle(gtoy1)) #> Validating the 'igraph' object... #> Vertex attribute 'name' missing; assigning names...  #> Creating a 'GraphSpace' object...  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{  # Example 1: Using RGraphSpace-native geoms # Edge clipping metadata are injected automatically ggplot(gs) +   geom_edgespace(colour = "red") +   geom_nodespace(aes(size = my_node_var),    fill = "steelblue", stroke = 2) +   scale_size(range = c(2, 15))  # Example 2: Mixing native and general geoms # Note possible clipping mismatch when combining # geom_edgespace() with generic ggplot2 node geoms. # Since geom_point() does not expose the final rendered # node radius to RGraphSpace, edge clipping is estimated # from layer parameters and may not exactly match the # displayed node geometry. ggplot(gs) +   geom_edgespace(colour = "red") +   geom_point(aes(x, y, size = my_node_var),    fill = "steelblue", stroke = 2, shape = 21) +   scale_size(range = c(2, 15))  } # }

title: Dynamic Scale Injection for Edge Clipping — inject_nodespace
text: function operates two stages within ggplot2 workflow: Capture: scans plot layers GeomNodeSpace extract mapping variables (aes()) static parameters (specifically size stroke). Injection: locates GeomEdgeSpace layers injects scale rules, captured mappings, fixed parameters geometry parameters. "lazy injection" calculates edge clipping based actual scales used nodes, even scales defined layers. Note: inject_nodespace() must called last ggplot chain allow function correctly scan previously added layers scales.

title: Dynamic Scale Injection for Edge Clipping — inject_nodespace
code: library(RGraphSpace) library(igraph) library(ggplot2)  # Generate a toy star graph gtoy1 <- make_star(15, mode="out")  # Set node and edge attributes V(gtoy1)$my_node_var <- runif(vcount(gtoy1), 1, 20) E(gtoy1)$my_edge_var <-  runif(ecount(gtoy1), 1, 20)  # Create a GraphSpace object with a circular layout gs <- GraphSpace(gtoy1, layout = layout_in_circle(gtoy1)) #> Validating the 'igraph' object... #> Vertex attribute 'name' missing; assigning names...  #> Creating a 'GraphSpace' object...  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Build the plot # Note that inject_nodespace() is called at the end to # synchronize node sizes with edge clipping. ggplot() +   geom_edgespace(aes(colour = my_edge_var), data = gs) +   geom_nodespace(aes(size = my_node_var), data = gs) +   scale_size(range = c(2, 15)) +   inject_nodespace() } # }
